content.text:
{He determines the number of the stars and calls them each by name.}{Psalm 147:4}
 {Mathematics is the science which uses easy words for hard ideas.}{— Edward Kasner}

A profound shift in perspective occurs when we view a system of linear 
equations not as a collection of constraints, but as a question about 
**vectors**. This viewpoint—thinking in terms of vectors rather 
than individual numbers—is the doorway to all of linear algebra.

content.title: Vectors in $\R^n$
content.content:
A **vector** in $\R^n$ is an ordered list of $n$ 
real numbers, written as a column:
\[
\vect{v} = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{bmatrix}
\]
The numbers $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$ are called the **components** 
or **entries** of $\vect{v}$.

The set of all vectors with $n$ components is denoted $\R^n$. Two 
vectors are **equal** if and only if their corresponding 
components are equal.

content.text:
When $n = 2$ or $n = 3$, we can visualize vectors as arrows in the 
plane or in space. The vector $

$ 
represents an arrow from the origin to the point $(3, 2)$.

content.title: Vector Operations
content.content:
Let $\vect{u}$ and $\vect{v}$ be vectors in $\R^n$ and let $c$ be a scalar. Then:

**Vector addition:**
\[
\vect{u} + \vect{v} = 
\begin{bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \\ \vdots \\ u_n \end{bmatrix} + 
\begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} u_1 + v_1 \\ u_2 + v_2 \\ \vdots \\ u_n + v_n \end{bmatrix}
\]

**Scalar multiplication:**
\[
c\vect{v} = c \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} cv_1 \\ cv_2 \\ \vdots \\ cv_n \end{bmatrix}
\]

content.text:
Geometrically, adding two vectors follows the **parallelogram rule**: 
place them tail-to-tail, complete the parallelogram, and the sum is the 
diagonal. Scalar multiplication stretches or shrinks the vector; negative 
scalars reverse its direction.

content.title: Linear Combination
content.content:
A **linear combination** of vectors 
$\vect{v}_1, \vect{v}_2, \ldots, \vect{v}_p$ with weights 
$c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_p$ is the vector
\[
c_1 \vect{v}_1 + c_2 \vect{v}_2 + \cdots + c_p \vect{v}_p
\]
The scalars $c_1, \ldots, c_p$ are called the **weights** of the 
linear combination.

content.title: Writing a vector as a linear combination
content.problem:
Can the vector $\vect{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}$ 
be written as a linear combination of 
$\vect{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix}$ and 
$\vect{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$?
content.solution:
We need to find scalars $x_1$ and $x_2$ such that 
$x_1 \vect{v}_1 + x_2 \vect{v}_2 = \vect{b}$:
\[
x_1 \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix} + 
x_2 \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}
\]
This vector equation is equivalent to the system:
$$
x_1 + 2x_2 &= 7 \\
-2x_1 + 5x_2 &= 4 \\
-5x_1 + 6x_2 &= -3
$$
Row reducing the augmented matrix:
\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c}
1 & 2 & 7 \\
-2 & 5 & 4 \\
-5 & 6 & -3
\end{array}\right]
\to
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c}
1 & 0 & 3 \\
0 & 1 & 2 \\
0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right]
\]
Yes! With $x_1 = 3$ and $x_2 = 2$, we have 
$\vect{b} = 3\vect{v}_1 + 2\vect{v}_2$.

content.text: The Span of a Set of Vectors

content.title: Span
content.content:
The **span** of vectors $\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_p$, 
denoted $\spn\{\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_p\}$, is the set of all 
linear combinations of these vectors:
\[
\spn\{\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_p\} = 
\{c_1 \vect{v}_1 + \cdots + c_p \vect{v}_p : c_1, \ldots, c_p \in \R\}
\]
We say that $\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_p$ **span** a set $H$ 
if $H = \spn\{\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_p\}$.

items: The span of one nonzero vector is a **line** through the origin.

The span of two non-parallel vectors in $\R^3$ is a **plane** 
 through the origin.

The span of three "truly different" vectors in $\R^3$ is all 
 of $\R^3$.

content.title: Span and Linear Systems
content.content:
A vector $\vect{b}$ is in $\spn\{\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_p\}$ if 
and only if the system with augmented matrix 
$[\vect{v}_1 \cdots \vect{v}_p | \vect{b}]$ is consistent.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This theorem bridges two perspectives: the geometric question "Is 
$\vect{b}$ in the span?" is equivalent to the algebraic question 
"Can we solve this system?"

marginNotes.text:
He is before all things, and in him 
all things hold together.

marginNotes.author: Hermann Weyl (1885--1955)
marginNotes.text:
Symmetry is one idea by which man 
through the ages has tried to comprehend and create order, beauty, and 
perfection.
